name: Recursive Math Library
scenario: You're building a 'pure math' utility library for a data science startup. The data scientists need recursively-defined mathematical functions: factorial, combinations, GCD, power, digit operations. They've specifically asked for recursive implementations because they want the code to MATCH the mathematical definitions — easier to verify mathematical correctness. Your job: build a comprehensive RecursiveMath utility class. After this project, you understand recursion deeply through varied applications.
description: Build a `RecursiveMath` class with multiple recursive methods covering common mathematical operations. Each method MUST use recursion (no loops). Each method demonstrates a different recursive pattern.

core.description: `power(int base, int exponent)` — returns base^exponent recursively
`gcd(int a, int b)` — returns greatest common divisor using Euclidean algorithm
`sumOfDigits(int n)` — returns sum of n's digits recursively
`countDigits(int n)` — returns number of digits in n recursively
`isPalindrome(int n)` — returns true if n is a palindrome (e.g., 12321) — use sumOfDigits-like decomposition
main() tests EVERY method with sample inputs and prints results

bonus.description: Add `fibonacci(int n)` with two base cases (n=0 returns 0, n=1 returns 1)
Add `isPrime(int n, int divisor)` — checks if n is prime by trying divisors recursively (starting at 2)
Add `multiply(int a, int b)` — multiplies WITHOUT using `*` operator (use repeated addition recursively)
Add `combinations(int n, int r)` — returns C(n,r) = n! / (r! * (n-r)!). Uses factorial method.

data_to_use:
Demonstrate each method with these inputs:
- factorial: 5, 10
- power: 2^10, 3^5
- gcd: (48, 36), (100, 75)
- sumOfDigits: 12345, 9876
- countDigits: 12345, 7
- isPalindrome: 12321 (true), 12345 (false)

Bonus inputs:
- fibonacci: 10
- isPrime: 7, 9
- multiply: 4 * 5
- combinations: C(5, 2) = 10

criteria: All methods are recursive — no loops (10 pts)
Recursive cases move toward base case (5 pts)
fibonacci with two base cases (10 pts)

ai_policy_explanation: Limited to 5 AI hints. Each method has a clear mathematical definition — translate the math to code. The trickiest one is isPalindrome — you might need a helper method or a clever approach. Think before coding.
submission_instructions: Save as RecursiveMath.java. Run it. Try inputs that would crash with iteration (factorial(20)) and try inputs that might crash with recursion (factorial(100000)). Push to GitHub portfolio.
tips_for_success: Write base case FIRST for every method. If you can't identify the base case, you don't understand the problem yet.
For each method, ask: 'how does this problem reduce to a smaller version of itself?'
For isPalindrome: reverse the number recursively, then compare original to reversed.
For countDigits: base case is n < 10 returns 1. Recursive: 1 + countDigits(n/10).
Trust the recursion — assume the recursive call works correctly. Don't trace 10 levels deep.
Test each method individually before adding the next. Build incrementally.
concepts_reinforced: Base case identification (different for each method)
Helper methods (for power, isPrime, multiply with accumulators)
what_youll_learn_by_doing_this: After 7+ recursive methods, recursion stops feeling magical and starts feeling natural. You see that every method has the same structure: base case + smaller version of itself. The variety of problems shows recursion's range — it's not just for factorial. By the end, you can look at a recursive mathematical definition and translate it to Java in 30 seconds. This skill matters in DSA, in competitive programming, and in any algorithm-heavy work.
extensions_for_advanced_learners: Add memoization to fibonacci (cache results in a class-level array)
Implement an iterative version of EACH method side-by-side; compare line counts
Try huge inputs and observe when StackOverflowError occurs
Build mergesort recursively (preview of DSA module)
